audit_status.source_base: Greek Romans text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Law marriage analogy, Law/sin distinction, commandment, divided I, wretched cry. Preserve Law as holy and sin as parasitic power.

verses.restored: Or do you not know, siblings, for I speak to those knowing Law, that the Law rules over the human for as long a time as one lives?
For the married woman has been bound by Law to the living husband; but if the husband dies, she has been released from the law of the husband.

So then, while the husband lives, she will be called adulteress if she comes to another man; but if the husband dies, she is free from the law, so that she is not adulteress, having come to another man.

So then, my siblings, you also were put to death to the Law through the body of Messiah, so that you might belong to another, to the one raised from the dead, so that we might bear fruit to the Divine.
For when we were in the flesh, the passions of sins/misalignments through the Law were working in our members to bear fruit to death.
But now we have been released from the Law, having died to that in which we were held, so that we serve in newness of Spirit and not in oldness of letter.

What then shall we say? Is the Law sin/misalignment? May it not be. But I would not have known sin/misalignment except through Law; for I would not have known desire if the Law had not said, You shall not desire.
But sin/misalignment, taking opportunity through the commandment, worked in me every desire; for apart from Law, sin/misalignment is dead.
I was once alive apart from Law, but when the commandment came, sin/misalignment came alive,
and I died; and the commandment that was into life, this was found to be into death for me.
For sin/misalignment, taking opportunity through the commandment, deceived me and through it killed me.
So then, the Law is holy, and the commandment holy and righteous/just and good.

Did the good become death to me? May it not be. But sin/misalignment, so that it might appear as sin/misalignment, was working death to me through the good, so that sin/misalignment through the commandment might become exceedingly sinful/misaligned.
For we know that the Law is spiritual; but I am fleshly, having been sold under sin/misalignment.
For what I work out, I do not know; for I do not practice what I will, but what I hate, this I do.
But if what I do not will, this I do, I agree with the Law that it is beautiful/good.
Now then it is no longer I working it out, but sin/misalignment dwelling in me.
For I know that good does not dwell in me, that is, in my flesh; for to will is present with me, but to work out the beautiful/good is not.
For the good that I will, I do not do; but the harmful thing I do not will, this I practice.
But if what I do not will, this I do, it is no longer I working it out, but sin/misalignment dwelling in me.
I find then the law/principle: when I will to do the beautiful/good, the harmful is present with me.
For I delight in the Law of the Divine according to the inner human,
but I see another law/principle in my members warring against the law/principle of my mind and taking me captive in the law/principle of sin/misalignment that is in my members.
Wretched human that I am. Who will rescue me from the body of this death?
Thanks to the Divine through Jesus Messiah our Lord. So then, I myself with the mind serve the Law of the Divine, but with the flesh the law/principle of sin/misalignment.

translation_notes: Romans 7 does not call Torah evil. The Law is holy; sin/misalignment is the parasitic power that exploits commandment.
The divided I may be read as rhetorical, autobiographical, Adamic, Israel-under-Law, or existential; the restored layer preserves the ambiguity.
